patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 1/4] mm/mempolicy: kernel_migrate_pages: simplify the usage of put_task_struct()
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: A patch to simplify the usage of put_task_struct() in kernel_migrate_pages, which has been questioned for potentially increasing task reference scope.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Raised a concern that the patch might increase task reference scope across do_migrate_pages(), but didn't object to it explicitly.

review_comments.author: Oleg Nesterov
review_comments.summary: Defended the patch, stating that it simplifies code and is necessary for future changes, but offered to rework it if needed.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 05/24, Gregory Price wrote:
>
> On Sun, May 24, 2026 at 06:57:59PM +0200, Oleg Nesterov wrote:
> >
> >  	mm = get_task_mm(task);
> > -	put_task_struct(task);
> > -
>
> Hm, I was a little trigger-happy on that review, I just realized that
> this increases the scope in which there is a task reference held (i.e.
> we're now holding a reference across do_migrate_pages())
>
> I don't *think* that's an issue, but it might we worth looking at the
> changelog to see if this was done intentionally.

I did this to simplify the code and the next changes. We pin this task's
memory across do_migrate_pages(), so I think that delaying put_task_struct()
doesn't add too much.

But of course, this is not necessary, I can rework if you don't like it.

Thanks for review!

Oleg.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [Linux Memory Hotness and Promotion] Notes from May 21, 2026
Re: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM)
Re: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM)

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH 1/4] mm/mempolicy: kernel_migrate_pages: simplify the usage of put_task_struct()

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils] nfsstat: add netlink support for fetching server statistics

version_history.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils] nfsstat: add netlink support for fetching server statistics

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds support for fetching NFS server statistics using Generic Netlink, a kernel feature that allows user-space applications to communicate with the kernel. When netlink is available, nfsstat sends a dump command and parses the reply to populate various stat arrays. If netlink is unavailable (e.g., on older kernels), it falls back to parsing /proc/net/rpc/nfsd as before. The patch requires corresponding kernel patches to add netlink support.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/4] nfsd/sunrpc: convert nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/4] nfsd/sunrpc: convert nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/4] sunrpc: add per-netns per-procedure call counts to svc_stat
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds per-network namespace (netns) and per-procedure call counts to the svc_stat structure in the Linux kernel's sunrpc module. The existing global call count arrays are not network-namespace-aware, so this change allows for namespace-scoped statistics to be returned through an upcoming netlink stats interface. The patch introduces a new vs_count pointer array in struct svc_stat and adds helper functions to manage per-version percpu call count arrays. It also updates the nfsd_net_init and nfsd_net_exit functions to allocate and free these counts as needed.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/4] sunrpc: use per-net counts in svc_seq_show()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the svc_seq_show() function to read procedure call counts from per-network-namespace arrays instead of global ones. This change allows /proc/net/rpc/nfsd to report procedure call counts for each network namespace separately. The patch only affects nfsd, which always allocates these counts via a specific initialization function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/4] nfsd: implement server-stats-get netlink handler
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a netlink handler to expose NFS server statistics, allowing them to be retrieved via the generic netlink family. The handler streams statistics across multiple messages, first sending scalar stats and then per-operation counts for NFSv4 procedures. This allows the nfsstat tool to retrieve server statistics using netlink, with a procfs fallback for older kernels.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 4/4] sunrpc: remove unused svc_version vs_count field
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the unused vs_count field from struct svc_version and its associated percpu counters in various NFS-related modules, as it is no longer accessed by any code. The removal of these fields simplifies the code and reduces memory usage.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series converts the nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink, allowing for per-network-namespace procedure call counts and exposing them through a new netlink handler in the nfsd generic netlink family. The procfs interface is fragile and has limitations, such as being global and not namespace-aware. This change adds per-netns counters to struct svc_stat and implements a server-stats-get netlink dump handler. It also removes unused global arrays and updates various files to use the new interfaces. The goal is to provide a more robust and extensible way for nfsstat to retrieve server statistics.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] support: mark unused arguments in netlink handlers with UNUSED()

version_history.subject: [PATCH] support: mark unused arguments in netlink handlers with UNUSED()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch marks unused arguments in netlink handlers with the UNUSED() macro to suppress -Wunused-parameter warnings. The affected functions are error_handler, finish_handler, and ack_handler. This change is a fix for recent compiler warnings.

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH] VFS: fix possible failure to unlock in nfsd4_create_file()
patches_acked.patch_summary: Fixes a potential failure to unlock in nfsd4_create_file() by addressing two possible issues related to atomic_open and dentry handling.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Identified two possible problems in nearby code that may be related to the patch. The patch itself looks correct and was reviewed by Jeff.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged Jeff's review and identified two potential issues with atomic_open. Provided context for the issues and explained that they are not necessarily critical.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, 25 May 2026, Jeff Layton wrote:
> On Mon, 2026-05-25 at 16:23 +1000, NeilBrown wrote:
> > atomic_create() in fs/namei.c drops the reference to the dentry
> > when it returns an error.
> > This behaviour was imported into dentry_create() so that it
> > will drop the reference if an error is returned from atomic_create(),
> > though not if vfs_create() returns an error (in the case where
> > ->atomic_create is not supported).
> > 
> > The caller - nfsd4_create_file() - is made aware of this by checking
> > path->dentry, which will either be a counted reference to a dentry, or
> > an error pointer.
> > 
> > However the change to use start_creating()/end_creating() (which landed
> > shortly before the dentry_create() change landed, though was likely
> > developed around the same time) means that nfsd4_create_file() *needs* a
> > valid dentry so that it can unlock the parent.
> > 
> > The net result is that if NFSD exports a filesystem which uses
> > ->atomic_create, and if a call to ->atomic_create returns an error, then
> > nfsd4_create_file() will pass an error pointer to end_creating()
> > and the parent will not be unlocked.
> > 
> > Fix this by changing dentry_create() to make sure path->dentry is always
> > a valid dentry, never an error-pointer.  The actual error is already
> > returned a different way.
> > 
> > Note that if ->atomic_create() returns a different dentry (which may not
> > be possible in practice) we are guaranteed (because it is only ever
> > provided by d_spliace_alias()) that it will have the same d_parent and
> > so it will have the same effect when passed to end_creating().
> > 
> > Fixes: 64a989dbd144 ("VFS/knfsd: Teach dentry_create() to use atomic_open()")
> > Signed-off-by: NeilBrown <neil@brown.name>
> > ---
> >  fs/namei.c | 10 ++++++++++
> >  1 file changed, 10 insertions(+)
> > 
> > diff --git a/fs/namei.c b/fs/namei.c
> > index c7fac83c9a85..4787244ca4a7 100644
> > --- a/fs/namei.c
> > +++ b/fs/namei.c
> > @@ -5024,6 +5024,7 @@ struct file *dentry_create(struct path *path, int flags, umode_t mode,
> >  {
> >  	struct file *file __free(fput) = NULL;
> >  	struct dentry *dentry = path->dentry;
> > +	struct dentry *orig_dentry = dentry;
> >  	struct dentry *dir = dentry->d_parent;
> >  	struct inode *dir_inode = d_inode(dir);
> >  	struct mnt_idmap *idmap;
> > @@ -5043,9 +5044,18 @@ struct file *dentry_create(struct path *path, int flags, umode_t mode,
> >  		if (create_error)
> >  			flags &= ~O_CREAT;
> >  
> > +		/* atomic_open will dput(dentry) on error */
> > +		dget(orig_dentry);
> >  		dentry = atomic_open(path, dentry, file, flags, mode);
> >  		error = PTR_ERR_OR_ZERO(dentry);
> >  
> > +		if (IS_ERR(dentry))
> > +			/* keep the original */
> > +			dentry = orig_dentry;
> > +		else
> > +			/* Drop the extra reference */
> > +			dput(orig_dentry);
> > +
> >  		if (unlikely(create_error) && error == -ENOENT)
> >  			error = create_error;
> >  
> 
> Sashiko noticed a couple of problems in nearby code that you may want
> to take a look at:
> 
>     https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/177969022571.3379282.16448744624428323496@noble.neil.brown.name?part=1
> 
> ...but the patch itself looks correct.
> 
> Reviewed-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> 

Thanks for forwarding that review.
It identifies two possible problems.  Both of them are hypothetically
possible based on the APIs.  One (involving atomic_open returning a
different dentry) would, I think, require a remote server that is
seriously misbehaving.  When asked to OPEN a file it would need to
return a filehandle for a directory that is already in the dcache.
The other (atomic_open doesn't actually open the file) appears to be
impossible with in-kernel filesystems as they only do that when the
dentry is already positive, and as nfsd has done a lookup while holding
the parent lock, that shouldn't be possible.  I haven't done a thorough
audit though and I may have missed a case.

The fixes are quite straight forward so I'll post patches to fix these
issues in nfsd.

I'm working on a rewrite of nfsd4_create_file and lookup_open() so that
nfsd can use a better interface which will improve the code and fix some
minor edge-case behavioural issues.  So I'm just focusing on simple
fixes at this stage, not necessarily best fixes.

Thanks,
NeilBrown

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 5/7 v3] mm/memcontrol: optimize memsw stock for cgroup v1

version_history.subject: [PATCH 5/7 v3] mm/memcontrol: optimize memsw stock for cgroup v1

series_items.subject: [PATCH 7/7 v3] mm/memcontrol: remove unused memcg_stock code
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes unused code related to handling memcg_stock in the Linux kernel's mm/memcontrol.c file. The code was moved to page_counter_stock, making it redundant and unnecessary. The removal of this code simplifies the kernel and reduces its complexity. The obj_stock functionality is left untouched as it is still needed. FLUSHING_CACHED_CHARGE is preserved for use with obj_stock.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 6/7 v3] mm/memcontrol: optimize stock usage for cgroup v2
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes memory usage in cgroup v2 by draining the stock of parent cgroups when a child is created, allowing other memcgs to reclaim the pages. This is necessary because direct charges to non-leaf cgroups are rare in cgroup v2, resulting in wasted memory in parent cgroups.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/7 v3] mm/memcontrol: convert memcg to use page_counter_stock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the memcg (memory cgroup) code to use page_counter_stock instead of its own stock handling logic. This change removes special casing for avoiding refilling and flushing of old stock, allows for failure during page_counter_enable_stock() if there is not enough memory to allocate a percpu page_counter_stock, and restructures drain_all_stock to iterate CPUs in the outer loop for better performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/7 v3] mm/page_counter: introduce stock drain APIs
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces two new APIs, page_counter_drain_stock_local() and page_counter_drain_stock_cpu(), to replace existing memcg stock draining functions. The local version takes a lock to serialize against concurrent charges, while the CPU version does not take a lock as it will only be called from the CPU hotplug path where no more charges can occur. These new APIs are designed to drain the stock of a page counter in a thread-safe manner.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/7 v3] mm/page_counter: use page_counter_stock in page_counter_try_charge
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the page_counter_try_charge function to be aware of stock limits. It first checks if there is enough stock available, and if so, charges from the stock without attempting a hierarchical charge. If there is not enough stock, it attempts a greedy hierarchical charge and then tries to add any surplus pages to the stock. The patch preserves the existing semantics for handling stock in try_charge_memcg.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/7 v3] mm/page_counter: introduce per-page_counter stock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a per-page_counter stock to improve memcg charging and limit checks, addressing issues such as random eviction of pages and tight coupling between stock and memcg.

review_comments.author: Sashiko
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential double-uncharging issue when disabling stock and suggested reordering the callsite to prevent it. Also noted that drain_all_stock() uses a mutex_trylock, which can fail.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 0/7 v3] mm/memcontrol, page_counter: move stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series moves the per-cpu stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter, consolidating stock limit checking and page_counter limit checking into page_counter_try_charge. This eliminates the 7-memcg-per-cpu slot limit, random evictions (drain & refill), and slot traversal. The resulting code in memcg is also easier to follow, as the caching becomes transparent from memcg's perspective and managed entirely within page_counter.

review_comments.author: Sashiko
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential double-uncharge issue when draining stock in disable_stock. Suggested reordering the callsite to prevent racy charges and ensure correct behavior.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the memsw stock for cgroup v1 by introducing an independent stock for the memsw page counter, which is charged separately from the memory page counter. This change improves caching and reduces the worst-case scenario where both counters exceed their limits, requiring a hierarchical charge. The user-visible effect is that reported memsw usage may temporarily be lower than memory usage due to batching of stock charges.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 1/7 v3] mm/page_counter: introduce per-page_counter stock

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/compaction: cap compact_gap() at COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch caps the `compact_gap()` function at `COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX` to prevent over-reserving memory and improve compaction success rates. The current implementation returns a gap that scales exponentially by order, but this can lead to unnecessary reclaim attempts and failed compaction checks. By capping the gap, the patch aims to align it with the actual capacity of the compaction scanner.

review_comments.author: Vlastimil Babka (SUSE)
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns that capping the `compact_gap()` function may mask genuine memory pressure and questioned the patch's impact on compaction success rates.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: skip high atomic reservation at or below costly order

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: skip high atomic reservation at or below costly order

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's page allocation mechanism to prevent high atomic reservations for allocations at or below a certain order (PAGE_ALLOC_COSTLY_ORDER). This is done by adding a check in the reserve_highatomic_pageblock function, which skips reserving entire pageblocks for smaller atomic allocations. The goal of this change is to reduce fragmentation and improve the success rate of large memory allocations, particularly for Transparent Huge Pages (THP) on systems with plenty of free memory.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Andrew Morton questioned whether skipping HighAtomic reservation for orders 1-3 would break anti-fragmentation guarantees, potentially leading to dropped packets or I/O errors in subsystems like the network stack or BTRFS.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sashiko asked

: Does skipping the HighAtomic reservation for orders 1-3 break the
: anti-fragmentation guarantees for these atomic allocations?
: 
: The MIGRATE_HIGHATOMIC reserve protects high-order atomic allocations
: from failing under fragmentation by taking ownership of the entire
: pageblock.
: 
: If order-1 through order-3 atomic allocations fall back to stealing
: pages, but the pageblock remains in its original migratetype, won't
: order-0 non-atomic allocations consume the remaining contiguous space?
: 
: Under memory pressure, this could leave no contiguous blocks for atomic
: allocations to steal.  Because these atomic allocations cannot trigger
: direct reclaim or compaction, they might fail, potentially leading to
: dropped packets or I/O errors in subsystems like the network stack or
: BTRFS.
: 
: Could background compaction or khugepaged be used to unreserve
: HighAtomic blocks dynamically instead of disabling the reserve for
: these orders?

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Johannes Weiner questioned the patch's effectiveness, noting that disabling highatomic reserve for costly orders didn't seem to matter and suggesting that maxing out reserves is unnecessary as highatomic allocations are typically short-lived.
review_comments.raw_body:
This is an interesting patch. A couple of thoughts:

1. You disabled the highatomic reserve for this workload and it didn't
seem to matter. Presumably <costly orders don't need the protection.

2. Maxing out the reserves is odd. ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC allocations will
try reserved space first, and I'd expect things that are commonly
highatomic to be short-lived. Why don't we stop with a couple of
claimed highatomic blocks that get continuously recycled?

3. The impact on THP and compaction success rate is pretty
extreme. How can 1% of memory throw such a wrench into the gears?

Have you tried this with other workloads?

review_comments.author: JP (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged reviewer's feedback that smaller atomic allocations do not benefit from the high atomic reservation, agreeing that it should be skipped for orders 1-3.

Author acknowledges that smaller atomic allocations do not reserve entire pageblocks, as observed in experiments where there were no failures for orders 1-3 on a fragmented system.

Author addressed Andrew Morton's concern that the patch would prevent smaller atomic allocations from being movable, explaining that the patch actually keeps these pageblocks movable and improves compaction

Author addressed a concern that reserved HighAtomic pageblocks are not treated as precious resources, explaining that under real memory pressure they are already given up by the kernel's unreserve mechanism.

Author responded to a concern about introducing additional overhead by explaining that the current patch already reduces reclaim and arguing against adding more scanning/overhead/stats.
review_comments.raw_body:
The data included in the changelog supports the claim that the reserve
does not provide a benefit at these orders. Even on fragmented systems,
orders 1-3 have plenty of pages available.

---

In the experiments, there were no failures for orders 1-3 despite a
fragmented system and no reserved pageblocks for these orders.

---

With the patch, these pageblocks stay movable. So if fallback is needed,
moveable pages can still be taken. But the patch actually improves
compaction so contiguous space is increased overall.

---

Reserved HighAtomic pageblocks are not currently treated as a precious
resource. Under real memory pressure, the kernel already gives them up -
the unreserve mechanism kicks in and converts the HighAtomic pageblocks
back to their original migrate type.

---

This would call for extra scanning/overhead/stats. The patch reduces
reclaim, for example. A new scanner feels like going in the opposite
direction.

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Vlastimil pointed out that the patch's logic for reserving high atomic pageblocks may not work as intended when an allocation succeeds before entering the slowpath, potentially leading to unnecessary reservation of additional blocks.

Reviewer Vlastimil suggested that instead of skipping high atomic reservations for low orders, the patch should focus on unreserving these reservations more proactively.

Reviewer noted that if all free memory is in HighAtomic blocks, compaction may not be effective and suggested a suitability check might be incorrectly assuming these blocks are usable
review_comments.raw_body:
Hmm, but if the allocation succeeds before entering slowpath,
ALLOC_NON_BLOCK won't be set.
But reserving another block should mean we already exhausted the reserved ones.
Unreserving is only done when direct reclaim made some progress but failed
to produce a page. But if it works, or kswapd does the job, we won't enter it?

---

Maybe it's some big burst of highatomic allocations that leads to the
reservations and then they stay around "forever"?

If that's the case I think we should be perhaps looking at the unreserving
being done more proactively, rather than limiting things to costly order.

---

Maybe if ~all free memory is in the highatomic blocks, compaction can't be
effective much. Or some suitability check somewhere in reclaim+compaction
wrongly assumes the highatomic blocks are usable, so it won't do the work.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 00/14] userfaultfd: working set tracking for VM guest memory

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 00/14] userfaultfd: working set tracking for VM guest memory

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 01/14] mm: decouple protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING by introducing a new configuration option, CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE. This allows architectures that support present-but-inaccessible page table entries (protnone PTEs) to use this capability independently of NUMA balancing. The patch updates various architecture-specific code and Kconfig files to reflect this change, enabling six architectures to select the new configuration option and making the protnone infrastructure available for other uses beyond NUMA balancing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 02/14] mm: rename uffd-wp PTE bit macros to uffd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the uffd-wp PTE bit macros to simply 'uffd' in various architectures, including x86 and arm64. The rename is done to reflect that the bit is used for userfaultfd tracking, regardless of whether it's write-protect or read-write-protect semantics are applied. This change does not affect behavior but rather updates naming conventions to be more accurate and consistent. The patch also includes corresponding changes in various header files across different architectures.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 03/14] mm: rename uffd-wp PTE accessors to uffd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames kernel-internal functions and variables related to the uffd-wp PTE bit, which is used to mark access-tracking PTEs in Userfaultfd RWP. The renaming is done to reflect that the bit's meaning now depends on the VMA flag (WP or RWP), not its name. This change does not affect behavior and is a purely mechanical rename. The patch updates various files across the kernel, including header files, source code, and documentation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 04/14] mm: add VM_UFFD_RWP VMA flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new VMA flag called VM_UFFD_RWP, which enables read-write protection for userfaultfd. The flag is introduced as part of the userfaultfd read-write protection (RWP) feature and is gated on the CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP configuration option. The patch also updates various kernel files to reserve the new flag and add helper functions for working with it. However, no code yet sets or queries the flag.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 05/14] mm: add MM_CP_UFFD_RWP change_protection() flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new change_protection() flags to the Linux kernel: MM_CP_UFFD_RWP and MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_RESOLVE. The MM_CP_UFFD_RWP flag is used to install a PAGE_NONE PTE with the uffd bit set, which distinguishes it from other faults such as mprotect(PROT_NONE) or NUMA hinting faults. The MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_RESOLVE flag is used to restore the original vma->vm_page_prot and clear the uffd bit. These flags are wired through various functions including change_pte_range(), change_huge_pmd(), and hugetlb_change_protection() to ensure that anon, shmem, THP, and hugetlb all share the same semantics. This patch is a preparatory step for userfaultfd RWP functionality.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 06/14] mm: preserve RWP marker across PTE rewrites
series_items.patch_summary: This patch ensures that the RWP (Read-Only Write-Protection) marker is preserved across PTE rewrites in various kernel paths, including swap, device-exclusive, migration, fork, and mprotect operations. The goal is to prevent the silent dropping of the PAGE_NONE semantics carried by the uffd bit when a VM_UFFD_RWP VMA is involved. The patch updates several functions to preserve the RWP marker during PTE rewrites, ensuring that the correct protection flags are maintained.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 07/14] mm: handle VM_UFFD_RWP in khugepaged, rmap, and GUP
series_items.patch_summary: This patch extends three mm paths to treat VM_UFFD_RWP (User FaultFD Read-Write Protected) the same as VM_UFFD_WP (User FaultFD Write Protected). The affected paths are khugepaged, rmap, and GUP. In khugepaged, ranges carrying the uffd PTE bit are now protected from collapse. In rmap, batching is capped at 1 for VM_UFFD_RWP to ensure each PTE is handled individually. In GUP, a fault is forced on VM_UFFD_RWP VMAs regardless of FOLL_HONOR_NUMA_FAULT. This ensures that RWP uses protnone as an access-tracking marker rather than for NUMA hinting.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 08/14] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT plumbing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to userfaultfd, allowing userspace to track read-write protection (RWP) on memory ranges. It introduces two new ioctls: UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT, which enable RWP tracking and installation/removal of RWP on registered ranges. The patch also adds a capability bit UFFD_FEATURE_RWP and config option CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP. The feature is not yet advertised in the API, but will be exposed in a follow-up patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 09/14] mm/userfaultfd: add RWP fault delivery and expose UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for read-write protection (RWP) in the Linux kernel's userfaultfd feature. It introduces a new fault delivery mechanism that notifies userspace when RWP-protected pages are accessed, allowing users to clear the protection using the UFFDIO_RWPROTECT ioctl. The patch also exposes the UFFD_FEATURE_RWP feature and adds support for anonymous, shmem, and hugetlb memory types. It works by modifying the fault handling code in various kernel modules, including huge_memory.c, hugetlb.c, and userfaultfd.c, to deliver RWP faults to userspace when necessary.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 10/14] mm/pagemap: add PAGE_IS_ACCESSED for RWP tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new category to the pagemap, called PAGE_IS_ACCESSED, which is used for read-write protection (RWP) tracking. It introduces a new bit in the PTE that indicates whether a page has been accessed, and scopes it to the VMA whose registered mode makes the bit meaningful. This allows for more precise tracking of RWP-protected pages and avoids conflation with write-protected pages. The patch also updates various files to reflect this change, including documentation and user API headers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 11/14] userfaultfd: add UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC for async fault resolution
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the userfaultfd system called UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC, which allows for asynchronous resolution of read-write page faults. This is useful for working-set tracking in virtual machines, where the kernel can restore access to pages without blocking the faulting thread. The VMM can then reconstruct the access pattern after the fact using PAGEMAP_SCAN. Additionally, this feature upgrades writable private anonymous PTEs when possible, preventing unnecessary copy-on-write faults.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 12/14] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_SET_MODE for runtime sync/async toggle
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new ioctl, UFFDIO_SET_MODE, which allows a VMM to toggle between sync and async RWP modes at runtime without re-registering the userfaultfd. The ioctl takes an enable/disable bitmask of UFFD_FEATURE_* flags, with only UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC being toggleable today. When switching to async mode, pending sync waiters are woken so they retry and auto-resolve under the new mode. This feature is useful for scenarios where a VMM needs to switch between sync and async modes on-the-fly, such as during working set scanning or page eviction.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 13/14] selftests/mm: add userfaultfd RWP tests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds userfaultfd (UFFD) read-write-protect (RWP) tests to the Linux kernel selftests suite. The tests cover various scenarios, including asynchronous and synchronous RWP modes, page mapping, mprotect operations, and fork behavior. The tests are designed to ensure that UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT work correctly in different memory types, such as anonymous, shared memory, and huge pages. The patch includes 11 test cases that verify the functionality of RWP under various conditions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 14/14] Documentation/userfaultfd: document RWP working set tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's userfaultfd feature, specifically covering read-write protection (RWP) working set tracking. It explains how RWP works, including its sync and async fault models, UFFDIO_RWPROTECT semantics, and UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC. The documentation also describes a typical workflow for VMM working-set-tracking, from detection to eviction and back to asynchronous mode.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds userfaultfd support for tracking the working set of VM guest memory, enabling a VMM to identify cold pages and evict them to tiered or remote storage. The approach involves introducing a new userfaultfd registration mode (RWP) that uses the same mechanism as existing modes but with additional features for read-write protection tracking.

review_comments.author: Mike Rapoport (Microsoft)
review_comments.summary: Reviewed-by: Mike Rapoport (Microsoft) <rppt@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)
review_comments.summary: Author provided additional context and clarification on the patch series, but no substantive review or feedback was given.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH man-pages v1 0/6] userfaultfd: document read-write protect mode

version_history.subject: [PATCH man-pages v1 0/6] userfaultfd: document read-write protect mode

series_items.subject: [PATCH man-pages v1 1/6] userfaultfd.2: Add read-write protect mode
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux man-pages for the userfaultfd system call, specifically for the read-write protect mode (UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP) introduced in Linux 7.2. The new feature allows user-space to receive page-fault notifications on any access (read or write) to a present page within a registered range, and provides options for asynchronous fault resolution. The patch updates the userfaultfd.2 man page to include descriptions of the new registration mode, pagefault flag, and VERSIONS line.

review_comments.author: Alejandro Colomar
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer pointed out that the description of UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP incorrectly states it traps every access to a present page, when in fact it only traps write accesses.

The reviewer requested that the patch include more useful hunk contexts, citing documentation in the CONTRIBUTING file and CONTRIBUTING.d directory.

The reviewer requested changes to the documentation formatting, specifically suggesting the use of \\[em] instead of \(em and semantic newlines to improve readability.

The reviewer pointed out two minor formatting issues in the man-page documentation: 'by default' should be followed by a comma, and the word 'until' should be moved to the next line due to semantic newlines.

The reviewer pointed out a minor grammar issue in the documentation, suggesting that 'user space' should be written as two words ('user-space') when used as a compound adjective.

The reviewer suggested reformatting a paragraph in the man-page documentation for better readability, specifically recommending that each sentence or clause should be on its own line to avoid ambiguity and improve comprehension.

Reviewer Alejandro Colomar suggested changing 'returns' to 'fails with/' in the documentation to accurately reflect that attempting to register both UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_WP on the same range results in an error.

The reviewer suggested renaming the 'read-write protect' mode to either 'read-write-protect' or 'read/write-protect' for consistency with the existing 'write-protect' mode.

Reviewer Alejandro Colomar noted that the patch uses .TP instead of .P for paragraph formatting, which is inconsistent with the rest of the man page, and suggested switching back to .P

Reviewer Alejandro Colomar requested that the patch be reformatted to have only one identifier per line, citing consistency as the reason.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Kiryl,

On 2026-05-25T13:28:11+0100, Kiryl Shutsemau wrote:

---

Please have a look at the CONTRIBUTING file, and the CONTRIBUTING.d
directory.  In particular, it would be nice to see more useful hunk
contexts, which can be done following this documentation:

<https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/docs/man-pages/man-pages.git/tree/CONTRIBUTING.d/git#n11>

---

Please use \[em] instead of \(em, and use spaces around them as if they
were parentheses --like this--.

Also, please use semantic newlines.  See man-pages(7):

$ MANWIDTH=64 man man-pages | awk '/Use semantic newlines/,/^$/'
   Use semantic newlines
     In  the  source  of a manual page, new sentences should be
     started on new lines, long sentences should be split  into
     lines at clause breaks (commas, semicolons, colons, and so
     on),  and  long  clauses  should be split at phrase bound‐
     aries.  This convention, sometimes known as "semantic new‐
     lines", makes it easier to  see  the  effect  of  patches,
     which  often operate at the level of individual sentences,
     clauses, or phrases.

That would be:

	When registered with
	.B UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP
	mode,
	user-space will receive a page-fault notification
	on any access
	\[em]read or write\[em]
	to a present page within the range.

BTW, about the last line, I think you may want to mean
s/present page/page present/, with inverted order of the words, that is,
the page is present within the range, right?

---

'By default' should be followed by a comma.  Also, because of semantic
newlines, I'd move 'until' to the next line.  Thus:

	By default,
	the faulted thread will be stopped from execution
	until user space removes the protection using a

---

We say user space, as two words.  'user-space' is used when it works as
a compound adjective (usual English rules).

---

Again semantic newlines:

	...
	was negotiated,
	the kernel restores access in place
	and the faulted thread continues without blocking.

A rule of thumb when separating lines semantically is that it should be
relatively easy to understand full lines without reading the surrounding
lines.

---

s/returns/fails with/

I user space, we transform the return value to an errno code, and return
-1.

---

This should probably be read-write-protect mode, for consistency with
write-prtect mode.  Or maybe read/write-protect mode.

---

We changed to using .P some years ago.  It works exactly the same, and
is easier to type.

---

This is weird again.  Please don't have more than one identifier per
line.


Have a lovely day!
Alex

series_items.subject: [PATCH man-pages v1 2/6] UFFDIO_RWPROTECT.2const: New page
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux man-pages project for the UFFDIO_RWPROTECT ioctl, which is used to install or remove read-write protection on a userfaultfd-registered memory range. The documentation covers the mode bits, populated-pages-only semantics, and error handling. It also includes an example of how to use the ioctl with a struct uffdio_rwprotect.

series_items.subject: [PATCH man-pages v1 3/6] UFFDIO_SET_MODE.2const: New page
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new manual page for the UFFDIO_SET_MODE ioctl, which allows userfaultfd feature bits to be toggled at runtime. The patch documents the uffdio_set_mode struct and its serialization against in-flight page faults, enabling a single userfaultfd to switch between async detection and synchronous eviction without re-registering its ranges.

series_items.subject: [PATCH man-pages v1 4/6] UFFDIO_API.2const: Document UFFD_FEATURE_RWP{,_ASYNC} and 1 << _UFFDIO_SET_MODE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the documentation for the UFFDIO_API(2const) man page to include two new feature bits: UFFD_FEATURE_RWP and UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC, which were introduced in Linux 7.2. These features enable read-write protection tracking and in-kernel resolution of RWP faults without notification. The patch also documents the ability to toggle this asynchronous mode at runtime using the UFFDIO_SET_MODE(2const) ioctl.

review_comments.author: Alejandro Colomar
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the documentation for UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC incorrectly states it requires UFFD_FEATURE_RWP to be set in the same UFFDIO_API call, when in fact they can be set independently.

Reviewer Alejandro Colomar pointed out that the comment about UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC requiring UFFD_FEATURE_RWP to be set in the same UFFDIO_API call is incomplete, as it does not mention what happens if this condition is not met.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Kiryl,

On 2026-05-25T13:28:14+0100, Kiryl Shutsemau wrote:

---

Have a lovely day!
Alex

review_comments.author: Alejandro Colomar
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Alejandro Colomar initially expressed concerns about splitting the patch into multiple parts but later reconsidered and agreed that it makes sense to submit them separately.
review_comments.raw_body:
Actually, I retire this.  I think it makes sense as a single patch.

Cheers,
Alex

series_items.subject: [PATCH man-pages v1 5/6] UFFDIO_REGISTER.2const: Document UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and 1 << _UFFDIO_RWPROTECT
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the documentation for the UFFDIO_REGISTER system call in the Linux kernel's man pages. Specifically, it adds descriptions for two new features: UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and 1 << _UFFDIO_RWPROTECT. The first feature allows tracking of every access (read or write) to a present page within a registered range, while the second feature is related to reporting notifications when the range has been protected with UFFDIO_RWPROTECT. The patch also clarifies compatibility with various file systems and error handling for invalid combinations of modes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH man-pages v1 6/6] ioctl_userfaultfd.2: Reference UFFDIO_RWPROTECT and UFFDIO_SET_MODE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the documentation for the ioctl_userfaultfd system call to include two new ioctls, UFFDIO_RWPROTECT and UFFDIO_SET_MODE, which were introduced in Linux 7.2. The patch adds these ioctls to the list of supported operations on a userfaultfd file descriptor.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series documents the userfaultfd read-write protect mode, a working-set tracking mechanism for VM guest memory introduced in Linux 7.2. The patches add new UAPI documentation for various ioctls and features related to this mode, including UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP, UFFD_FEATURE_RWP, and UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC. The series also updates the userfaultfd manual page to include an overview of the read-write protect mode.

review_comments.author: Alejandro Colomar
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer pointed out that the documentation for UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP does not mention the required capability bit UFFD_FEATURE_RWP

Reviewer Alejandro Colomar requested that the patch author improve the hunk contexts in the CONTRIBUTING file, specifically suggesting to follow the documentation provided in the CONTRIBUTING.d/git file.

Alejandro Colomar requested changes to the formatting of a specific sentence in the man-page documentation, suggesting the use of semantic newlines and proper spacing around inline emphasis tags.

Reviewer Alejandro Colomar requested that 'by default' be followed by a comma and suggested reformatting the sentence to move 'until' to the next line due to semantic newlines.

The reviewer pointed out that the patch uses inconsistent terminology for referring to user space, suggesting that it should be written as two words ('user space') instead of a hyphenated compound adjective ('user-space').

Reviewer Alejandro Colomar suggested reformatting the man-page text to improve readability, specifically recommending that semantic newlines should be used in a way that allows full lines to be understood without reading surrounding lines.

Reviewer Alejandro Colomar suggested changing 'returns' to 'fails with/' in the documentation to accurately reflect that the userfaultfd ioctl fails with an error code when the read-write protect mode is not supported

Reviewer Alejandro Colomar suggested renaming the 'read-write protect' mode to either 'read-write-protect', 'read/write-protect', or 'read-write protect' for consistency with existing terminology.

Reviewer Alejandro Colomar pointed out that the patch should use the `.P` macro instead of the original method for formatting, citing ease of typing as a benefit.

Reviewer Alejandro Colomar pointed out that the patch has multiple identifiers on each line, which is inconsistent and requested to follow the convention of having only one identifier per line.

     lines at clause breaks (commas, semicolons, colons, and so
     on),  and  long  clauses  should be split at phrase bound‐
     aries.  This convention, sometimes known as "semantic new‐

That would be:

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about addressing existing issues before adding new features to the patch series. Suggested that it would be better to address these issues immediately and then base the new feature-add on top.

hm.  What's this.

Sashiko seems to have been useful this time around.

There are quite a few like this.  Would it be better to address these
existing issues immediately then base the new feature-add on top?

> 
> > To: sashiko-reviews@lists.linux.dev

sent to the list. I've replied to all recipients of the patchset not
only to kvm@.

Yeah, better than last time.

> > patch on top of this series.
> 
> There are quite a few like this.  Would it be better to address these
> existing issues immediately then base the new feature-add on top?

Fair enough. Will prepare patches this week.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] perf/ftrace: Fix WARNING in __unregister_ftrace_function
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a warning that occurs when trying to unregister an ftrace function that was never successfully registered. The fix involves checking if the ftrace_ops is enabled before attempting to unregister it, and if not, simply freeing the filter and returning success. This prevents a WARN_ON condition from being triggered during error cleanup in perf_event_alloc(). The patch modifies the perf_ftrace_function_unregister() function to include this check.

review_comments.author: Masami (Google)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where the ftrace_ops is already torn down by perf_try_init_event()'s err_destroy path, and suggested adding a check for this condition.

Reviewer Masami acknowledged the patch as correct and suitable for inclusion
review_comments.raw_body:

Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com> wrote:

---

Looks good to me.

Acked-by: Masami Hiramatsu (Google) <mhiramat@kernel.org>
Fixes: ced39002f5ea ("ftrace, perf: Add support to use function tracepoint in perf")

Thanks,

review_comments.author: Rik Riel (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author responded to Steven Rostedt's concern that calling ftrace_free_filter() twice could cause issues, explaining that it sets the ->*_hash values to EMPTY_HASH and thus wouldn't lead to problems.

Author acknowledged that the error handling in perf_event_alloc() error cleanup path needs to be restructured, and agreed to do so in v2.

Author is unsure about the specific code being referenced by Sashiko, and asks if they are looking at a different tree than upstream Linus.

review_comments.author: Masami (Google)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where perf_ftrace_function_unregister() is called on an event whose ftrace_ops registration failed, and suggested adding a check for FTRACE_OPS_FL_ENABLED before unregistering

Reviewer Masami noted that the commit being reviewed is too old and not used anymore, requesting further investigation from Sashiko.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/4] memcg: shrink obj_stock_pcp and cache multiple objcgs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/4] memcg: store node_id instead of pglist_data pointer
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series aims to optimize memory cgroup (memcg) performance by replacing the use of a pointer to pglist_data in struct obj_stock_pcp with an int16_t node_id. This change reduces memory usage on 64-bit machines, where the pointer costs 8 bytes. The patch also includes a BUILD_BUG_ON() to ensure that MAX_NUMNODES does not exceed S16_MAX.

review_comments.author: Qi Zheng
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with an Acked-by. No specific technical comments or concerns were raised.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 5/22/26 9:19 AM, Shakeel Butt wrote:
> The struct obj_stock_pcp stores a pointer to pglist_data for the slab
> stats cached on the cpu. On 64-bit machines, this costs 8 bytes. The
> pointer is not strictly required: NODE_DATA() can recover it from the
> node id. Replace cached_pgdat with int16_t node_id and use NUMA_NO_NODE
> as the "no stats cached" sentinel.
> 
> At the moment all the archs limit MAX_NUMNODES to 1024 so int16_t is
> plenty; a BUILD_BUG_ON() makes sure we notice if that ever changes.
> 
> Fixes: 01b9da291c49 ("mm: memcontrol: convert objcg to be per-memcg per-node type")
> Tested-by: kernel test robot <oliver.sang@intel.com>
> Signed-off-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
> Acked-by: Muchun Song <muchun.song@linux.dev>
> Reviewed-by: Harry Yoo (Oracle) <harry@kernel.org>
> ---
> 
> Changes since v1:
> - Added tags in the commit message
> 
>   mm/memcontrol.c | 26 +++++++++++++++++++-------
>   1 file changed, 19 insertions(+), 7 deletions(-)
> 

Acked-by: Qi Zheng <qi.zheng@linux.dev>

Thanks!

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/4] memcg: uint16_t for nr_bytes in obj_stock_pcp
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the 'nr_bytes' field in the 'struct obj_stock_pcp' from an unsigned int to a uint16_t to reduce memory usage on per-CPU caches. The change is necessary because the current implementation can overflow on larger page sizes, such as 256KB pages. To prevent this, the patch accumulates new totals in an unsigned int local variable and flushes whenever the accumulator would hit U16_MAX. Additionally, it uses atomic_add to push any sub-page remainder above U16_MAX into objcg->nr_charged_bytes for configurations with PAGE_SHIFT > 16.

review_comments.author: Qi Zheng
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Qi Zheng suggested changing the refill condition in __refill_obj_stock() to prevent refilling more than U16_MAX bytes, which would cause nr_bytes to overflow.
review_comments.raw_body:
^

should also be changed to: don't refill > U16_MAX ?

Otherwise:

Acked-by: Qi Zheng <qi.zheng@linux.dev>

Thanks!

review_comments.author: Muchun Song
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the patch needs further revision and promised to either fix the issue in a new version or request Andrew to make an in-place change.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks. If I send a new version, I will fix this otherwise I will ask Andrew to
fix this inplace.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/4] memcg: int16_t for cached slab stats
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series aims to optimize memory cgroup (memcg) performance by reducing the size of cached slab statistics from 4 bytes to 2 bytes per counter on 64-bit machines. The change involves switching struct obj_stock_pcp's 'int' counters to 'int16_t', which will shrink the cached metadata.

review_comments.author: David Laight
review_comments.summary: Suggested modifying the order of operations in __account_obj_stock() to first add nr to *bytes, then check if the result is within S16_MAX and PAGE_SIZE bounds. If not, flush directly via mod_objcg_mlstate().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 4/4] memcg: multi objcg charge support
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a performance regression caused by the recent change to split a memcg's single obj_cgroup into one per NUMA node. The patch introduces multi-slot support for obj_stock_pcp, which caches cached_objcg variants of one memcg plus a few siblings without forcing a drain. This is achieved by turning nr_bytes and cached_objcg into arrays, scanning all slots on consume/refill/account, and evicting a random slot only when full. The patch also optimizes the layout to minimize cache line touches.

review_comments.author: Muchun Song
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Muchun Song requested that the patch author update the comment numbering to match the actual sequence, noting that it was incorrect.

Reviewer Muchun Song noted that the patch does not handle the case where a CPU has multiple objcg variants of the same memcg, and requested that the patch be modified to handle this scenario.

review_comments.raw_body:
Haha I didn't even notice. If I send a new version, I will fix this otherwise I
will ask Andrew to fix inplace.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a performance regression caused by the introduction of per-node obj_cgroup in commit 01b9da291c49. The regression occurs when threads of the same memcg run on different NUMA nodes, causing the per-CPU obj_stock_pcp to thrash. To fix this, the patches shrink the existing single-slot obj_stock_pcp fields and convert cached_objcg and nr_bytes into slot arrays using a multi-slot pattern already used by memcg_stock_pcp. This approach allows multiple objcgs per CPU to coexist in the stock without forcing a drain, making it more efficient than the temporary fix introduced earlier.

review_comments.author: Qi Zheng
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Qi Zheng noted that the patch should change the condition for refilling obj_stock_pcp to avoid overflowing a uint16_t, and acknowledged the patch with an Acked-by tag.

review_comments.author: David Laight
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer David Laight noted that the patch does not handle the case where the obj_stock_pcp is already locked, which could lead to a deadlock when trying to acquire the lock again in reclaim paths.

Reviewer David Laight suggested reordering the code to first add bytes to the cache and then check if it can be stored in a single slot, otherwise calling mod_objcg_mlstate() to update the object's memory layout state.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] memcg: use round-robin victim selection in refill_stock

version_history.subject: [PATCH] memcg: use round-robin victim selection in refill_stock

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch replaces the random victim selection in memcg's refill_stock function with a round-robin approach, addressing safety concerns related to NMI contexts and reentrancy.

review_comments.author: Harry Yoo
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch, noting that it fixes a safety issue and affects the latest LTS version.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 5/22/26 7:37 AM, Shakeel Butt wrote:
> Harry Yoo reported that get_random_u32_below() is not safe to call in
> the nmi context and memcg charge draining can happen in nmi context.
> 
> More specifically get_random_u32_below() is neither reentrant- nor
> NMI-safe: it acquires a per-cpu local_lock via local_lock_irqsave() on
> the batched_entropy_u32 state. An NMI that lands on a CPU mid-update of
> the ChaCha batch state and recurses into the random subsystem would
> corrupt that state. The memcg_stock local_trylock prevents re-entry
> on the percpu stock itself, but cannot protect an unrelated
> subsystem's per-cpu lock.
> 
> Replace the random pick with a per-cpu round-robin counter stored in
> memcg_stock_pcp and serialized by the same local_trylock that already
> guards cached[] and nr_pages[]. No atomics, no random calls, no extra
> locks needed.
> 
> Fixes: f735eebe55f8f ("memcg: multi-memcg percpu charge cache")

Acked-by: Harry Yoo (Oracle) <harry@kernel.org>

and perhaps

Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>

as it affects v6.18 (the latest LTS).

Thanks a lot for fixing it, Shakeel!

> Reported-by: Harry Yoo <harry@kernel.org>
> Closes: https://lore.kernel.org/4e20f643-6983-4b6e-b12d-c6c4eb20ae0c@kernel.org/
> Signed-off-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
> ---
>   mm/memcontrol.c | 5 ++++-
>   1 file changed, 4 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)
> 
> diff --git a/mm/memcontrol.c b/mm/memcontrol.c
> index 0eb50e639f0a..6392a2704441 100644
> --- a/mm/memcontrol.c
> +++ b/mm/memcontrol.c
> @@ -2031,6 +2031,7 @@ struct memcg_stock_pcp {
>   
>   	struct work_struct work;
>   	unsigned long flags;
> +	uint8_t drain_idx;
>   };
>   
>   static DEFINE_PER_CPU_ALIGNED(struct memcg_stock_pcp, memcg_stock) = {
> @@ -2214,7 +2215,9 @@ static void refill_stock(struct mem_cgroup *memcg, unsigned int nr_pages)
>   	if (!success) {
>   		i = empty_slot;
>   		if (i == -1) {
> -			i = get_random_u32_below(NR_MEMCG_STOCK);
> +			i = stock->drain_idx++;
> +			if (stock->drain_idx == NR_MEMCG_STOCK)
> +				stock->drain_idx = 0;
>   			drain_stock(stock, i);
>   		}
>   		css_get(&memcg->css);

-- 
Cheers,
Harry / Hyeonggon

review_comments.author: Michal Hocko
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch, thanking Shakeel for fixing a safety issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu 21-05-26 15:37:51, Shakeel Butt wrote:
> Harry Yoo reported that get_random_u32_below() is not safe to call in
> the nmi context and memcg charge draining can happen in nmi context.
> 
> More specifically get_random_u32_below() is neither reentrant- nor
> NMI-safe: it acquires a per-cpu local_lock via local_lock_irqsave() on
> the batched_entropy_u32 state. An NMI that lands on a CPU mid-update of
> the ChaCha batch state and recurses into the random subsystem would
> corrupt that state. The memcg_stock local_trylock prevents re-entry
> on the percpu stock itself, but cannot protect an unrelated
> subsystem's per-cpu lock.
> 
> Replace the random pick with a per-cpu round-robin counter stored in
> memcg_stock_pcp and serialized by the same local_trylock that already
> guards cached[] and nr_pages[]. No atomics, no random calls, no extra
> locks needed.
> 
> Fixes: f735eebe55f8f ("memcg: multi-memcg percpu charge cache")
> Reported-by: Harry Yoo <harry@kernel.org>
> Closes: https://lore.kernel.org/4e20f643-6983-4b6e-b12d-c6c4eb20ae0c@kernel.org/
> Signed-off-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

Acked-by: Michal Hocko <mhocko@suse.com>

Thanks!

> ---
>  mm/memcontrol.c | 5 ++++-
>  1 file changed, 4 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)
> 
> diff --git a/mm/memcontrol.c b/mm/memcontrol.c
> index 0eb50e639f0a..6392a2704441 100644
> --- a/mm/memcontrol.c
> +++ b/mm/memcontrol.c
> @@ -2031,6 +2031,7 @@ struct memcg_stock_pcp {
>  
>  	struct work_struct work;
>  	unsigned long flags;
> +	uint8_t drain_idx;
>  };
>  
>  static DEFINE_PER_CPU_ALIGNED(struct memcg_stock_pcp, memcg_stock) = {
> @@ -2214,7 +2215,9 @@ static void refill_stock(struct mem_cgroup *memcg, unsigned int nr_pages)
>  	if (!success) {
>  		i = empty_slot;
>  		if (i == -1) {
> -			i = get_random_u32_below(NR_MEMCG_STOCK);
> +			i = stock->drain_idx++;
> +			if (stock->drain_idx == NR_MEMCG_STOCK)
> +				stock->drain_idx = 0;
>  			drain_stock(stock, i);
>  		}
>  		css_get(&memcg->css);
> -- 
> 2.53.0-Meta

-- 
Michal Hocko
SUSE Labs
